item.title: The Bone-Chilling Story Of Pamela Smart
item.description:
Pamela Smart was convicted in 1991 for her role in the 1990 killing of her husband, Gregg Smart, in Derry, New Hampshire. Prosecutors said the crime was carried out by her teenage partner and his friends after she influenced them to commit the act.
At the time, Smart was working at a high school where she became involved with a 15 year old student. Investigators said this relationship became central to the case, as testimony and recorded conversations were used to support claims of a planned attack.
She was found guilty of being an accomplice to first degree murder, along with conspiracy and witness tampering, and was sentenced to life in prison without parole. The case received national attention and remains widely discussed decades later.

media:content.media:title: The Bone-Chilling Story Of Pamela Smart
media:content.media:description:
Pamela Smart was convicted in 1991 for her role in the 1990 killing of her husband, Gregg Smart, in Derry, New Hampshire. Prosecutors said the crime was carried out by her teenage partner and his friends after she influenced them to commit the act.
At the time, Smart was working at a high school where she became involved with a 15 year old student. Investigators said this relationship became central to the case, as testimony and recorded conversations were used to support claims of a planned attack.
She was found guilty of being an accomplice to first degree murder, along with conspiracy and witness tampering, and was sentenced to life in prison without parole. The case received national attention and remains widely discussed decades later.

item.title: The Twisted Crimes of the Hillside Stranglers of Los Angeles
item.description:
Between 1977 and 1978, a series of killings in Los Angeles were linked to two cousins, Kenneth Bianchi and Angelo Buono. The victims, mostly young women, were abducted and later found in hillside areas, leading to the name “Hillside Stranglers.”
The case caused widespread fear as investigators worked through thousands of leads. Authorities later determined the pair used deception, including posing as police officers, to gain victims’ trust before the crimes.
Bianchi was arrested in 1979 and later testified against Buono as part of a plea deal. Both were convicted and received life sentences. The case remains one of the most well known serial crime investigations in Los Angeles history.

media:content.media:title: The Twisted Crimes of the Hillside Stranglers of Los Angeles
media:content.media:description:
Between 1977 and 1978, a series of killings in Los Angeles were linked to two cousins, Kenneth Bianchi and Angelo Buono. The victims, mostly young women, were abducted and later found in hillside areas, leading to the name “Hillside Stranglers.”
The case caused widespread fear as investigators worked through thousands of leads. Authorities later determined the pair used deception, including posing as police officers, to gain victims’ trust before the crimes.
Bianchi was arrested in 1979 and later testified against Buono as part of a plea deal. Both were convicted and received life sentences. The case remains one of the most well known serial crime investigations in Los Angeles history.
